# Phase 8 — Flask-Migrate (Alembic) integration

**Status:** Draft for review
**Date:** 2026-05-28
**Scope:** Introduce schema-migration infrastructure to cancelchain via Flask-Migrate (the Pallets-aligned wrapper around Alembic). Add a tracked `migrations/` directory with an autogenerated initial migration covering the current schema. Wire `Migrate(app, db)` into `create_app()`. Change `cancelchain init` to call `flask_migrate.upgrade()` so the canonical schema-creation path is the migration history. Add `cancelchain db check` as a fifth step in `.github/workflows/tests.yml` (after the existing `ruff check`, `ruff format --check`, `pytest`, and `mypy` steps) so model edits without a matching migration go red immediately. Set a `MetaData(naming_convention=...)` on `Base.metadata` so future-generated migrations get stable, predictable constraint names. Closes the "Phase 7+ — Alembic migration framework" entry on the ROADMAP, originally surfaced during Phase 3 as "before going to prod prevents pain later."

## Goal

Make schema changes safe, reviewable, and reversible from this point forward. Cancelchain is 100% greenfield — no production deployment, no installed-base data, no existing SQLite files in the wild. Today, the only way to apply a model change is `db.create_all()` (run by `cancelchain init` against a fresh DB), which works fine right now but offers no diff/rollback story the moment we want to evolve the schema without wiping the DB. Phase 8 establishes the migration framework while we still have the luxury of treating the initial migration as a clean starting point — no `alembic stamp head` ceremony for installed databases, no renaming of pre-existing constraints, no production coordination. Every future schema change goes through `cancelchain db migrate` + a reviewable migration script under version control.

## Non-goals

- **No deployment-related infrastructure.** Cancelchain has no production deployment and no installed-base data anywhere. Phase 8 ships the migration framework; downstream deployment automation (CI pipelines that run migrations on push, rollback playbooks, blue/green migration coordination) is out of scope and waits until there's a real deployment to coordinate with.
- **No schema changes in this PR.** The initial migration captures the *current* schema exactly. Any schema modification rides on a follow-up PR.
- **No raw Alembic — Flask-Migrate.** The codebase is Flask-centric (FlaskGroup CLI, Flask-SQLAlchemy `model_class=Base`, blueprint views, `create_app()`). Raw Alembic would require us to write the Flask-aware `env.py` ourselves and add a CLI wrapper around `alembic` that ends up looking a lot like Flask-Migrate. Flask-Migrate is the path of least friction.
- **No `MappedAsDataclass` or other SA features.** Phase 7b kept `DeclarativeBase` plain; this spec doesn't change that.
- **No test-suite migration runs.** Tests stay on `db.create_all()` for speed (~50s suite vs. running ~all migrations per test invocation). Production / `cancelchain init` use migrations. The CI `cancelchain db check` gate enforces that the two stay equivalent at the model level.
- **No `cancelchain db ...` subcommand wiring.** Flask-Migrate registers `flask db ...` commands with Flask's CLI system; `FlaskGroup` auto-discovers them, so `cancelchain db migrate` / `cancelchain db upgrade` / `cancelchain db current` etc. work out of the box without additional CLI code.

## Decisions taken during brainstorming

- **`cancelchain init` calls `alembic upgrade head`.** Single source of truth: the migration history. Tests still use `db.create_all()` (test fixtures aren't going to run migrations on every session). Rejected: keeping `init` as `db.create_all()` separately from migrations (long-term silent drift between `db.metadata` and the migration history); replacing `init` entirely with `db upgrade` (the rename adds churn without benefit when both forms can coexist with `init` as the shorthand).
- **CI gate `cancelchain db check` is in.** Adds friction (a model edit without a matching migration goes red in CI), but that friction IS the value — it's what enforces the "single source of truth" property. Acknowledged trade-off: tighter discipline required of contributors.
- **`naming_convention` on `Base.metadata` is in.** Without it, auto-generated migrations carry SA-default constraint names which can change across SA versions. The cancelchain naming convention follows Alembic's recommendation (`ix_`, `uq_`, `ck_`, `fk_`, `pk_` prefixes). Acknowledged trade-off: the initial migration captures the new canonical names rather than the existing SA-default names — fine for cancelchain (no shipped DB, no installed constraints to rename).
- **Hand-review the auto-generated initial migration as an explicit step in the impl plan.** Alembic's `--autogenerate` is good but not perfect (misses CHECK constraints, server defaults sometimes, type-flavor differences). A manual diff against `db.metadata` before committing the initial migration is a small safeguard against the framework swallowing something.
- **Flask-Migrate version pin: `>=4`.** Flask-Migrate's `db check` subcommand (exposed in our CLI as `cancelchain db check`) was added in Flask-Migrate 4.0 (2023). 4.x is current and stable; no reason to pin below.
- **`migrations/` is tracked, not gitignored.** Migration scripts are the source of truth for the schema; losing them == losing history.

## Architecture

### Component map

| File | Role | Phase 8 change |
|---|---|---|
| `src/cancelchain/database.py` | Defines `Base(DeclarativeBase)` + `db = SQLAlchemy(model_class=Base)` | Add `MetaData(naming_convention=...)` to `Base.metadata` (set on the `Base` class via `metadata = MetaData(naming_convention=...)`). |
| `src/cancelchain/__init__.py` | `create_app()` factory | Add `from flask_migrate import Migrate` import and one new line: `Migrate(app, db)` after `db.init_app(app)`. |
| `src/cancelchain/command.py` | CLI commands including `init` | Change `init_db_command` body from `db.create_all()` to `flask_migrate.upgrade()` (programmatic call equivalent to `flask db upgrade head`). |
| `pyproject.toml` | Project dependencies | Add `Flask-Migrate>=4` to `[project.dependencies]` (capitalized to match the existing `Flask`, `Flask-Caching`, `Flask-SQLAlchemy` entries). |
| `uv.lock` | Reproducible build lock | Regenerated by `uv lock` after the pyproject change. |
| `migrations/` (NEW) | Alembic environment + initial migration | Created by `cancelchain db init`, then edited minimally to align with project style. |
| `.github/workflows/tests.yml` | CI gates | Add `uv run cancelchain db check` as a fifth step after the existing `ruff check`, `ruff format --check`, `pytest`, and `mypy` steps. |

### `Base.metadata` naming convention (in `database.py`)

Alembic-recommended convention, set once on `Base.metadata`:

```python
from sqlalchemy import MetaData

_NAMING_CONVENTION = {
    'ix': 'ix_%(column_0_label)s',
    'uq': 'uq_%(table_name)s_%(column_0_name)s',
    'ck': 'ck_%(table_name)s_%(constraint_name)s',
    'fk': 'fk_%(table_name)s_%(referred_table_name)s_%(column_0_name)s',
    'pk': 'pk_%(table_name)s',
}


class Base(DeclarativeBase):
    metadata = MetaData(naming_convention=_NAMING_CONVENTION)
```

After this, auto-generated migrations carry stable names like `ix_block_idx` instead of the SA-default `ix_block_idx_PASSTHRU_HASH`.

### `Migrate(app, db)` wiring (in `__init__.py`)

The `create_app()` factory gets one new line after `db.init_app(app)`:

```python
from flask_migrate import Migrate
...
def create_app() -> Flask:
    ...
    db.init_app(app)
    Migrate(app, db)  # registers `flask db ...` commands
    ...
```

That's enough for `cancelchain db migrate` / `db upgrade` / `db check` / `db current` to work. No additional Click code needed — `FlaskGroup` auto-discovers what Flask-Migrate registers.

### `cancelchain init` change (in `command.py`)

Current (`command.py:352-359`):
```python
@click.command('init', help='Initialize the database.')
@with_appcontext
def init_db_command() -> None:
    try:
        db.create_all()
        console.print('Initialized the database.', style='success')
    except Exception as e:
        console.print(f'Initialization failed: {e}', style='error')
```

After:
```python
from flask_migrate import upgrade as flask_migrate_upgrade

@click.command('init', help='Initialize the database.')
@with_appcontext
def init_db_command() -> None:
    try:
        flask_migrate_upgrade()  # runs `alembic upgrade head` programmatically
        console.print('Initialized the database.', style='success')
    except Exception as e:
        console.print(f'Initialization failed: {e}', style='error')
```

The error message ("Initialized the database.") still fits — from a user's perspective, `cancelchain init` still creates a working DB. The mechanism just changed from "create_all" to "run every migration in sequence."

### `migrations/` directory layout

After `cancelchain db init`:

```
migrations/
├── alembic.ini
├── env.py                            (auto-generated, may need minor edits)
├── script.py.mako                    (auto-generated, no edits needed)
└── versions/
    └── <hex>_initial_schema.py       (created by `cancelchain db migrate -m "initial schema"`)
```

`env.py` typically needs one edit to wire `target_metadata = db.metadata` (Flask-Migrate's `flask db init` template often does this correctly, but verify). `alembic.ini` may want minor edits to align with project style (log levels, file pattern). The `migrations/versions/` directory holds all future revisions — `<hex>_initial_schema.py` is the first.

### CI gate addition

The existing `.github/workflows/tests.yml` runs four steps (`ruff check`, `ruff format --check`, `pytest`, `mypy`). Phase 8 adds a fifth:

```yaml
- env:
    FLASK_SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI: sqlite://
  run: uv run cancelchain db check
```

`cancelchain db check` (Flask-Migrate 4.0+ via the FlaskGroup auto-discovery) compares `Base.metadata` to the schema implied by `alembic upgrade head` and exits non-zero if they differ. Catches the case where someone edits a model without generating a migration.

The `cancelchain` CLI provides the app context through `FlaskGroup(create_app=create_app)`. `create_app()` calls `db.init_app(app)`, which raises a `RuntimeError` when `SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI` is unset — but the factory **catches and logs** the error (see `src/cancelchain/__init__.py:64-67`) so app construction succeeds anyway with `db` left unbound. The downstream `cancelchain db check` then fails when Flask-Migrate tries to use the unbound `db` extension. Setting `FLASK_SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI=sqlite://` (in-memory SQLite, never written) lets `db.init_app(app)` succeed cleanly so the check has a usable extension — `app.config.from_prefixed_env()` picks the `FLASK_*` env var up automatically. The check itself never opens the DB; it builds the migration-implied schema in-process and compares to `Base.metadata`. Same env var works for local runs.

## Changes

### Files

- **Create:** `migrations/` directory with `alembic.ini`, `env.py`, `script.py.mako`, and `versions/<hex>_initial_schema.py`. Created by `cancelchain db init` + `cancelchain db migrate -m "initial schema"`, then minor edits + hand-review.
- **Modify:** `src/cancelchain/database.py` — add `MetaData(naming_convention=...)` import and assignment on `Base.metadata`. 1 new import line + ~8 new lines for the dict + assignment.
- **Modify:** `src/cancelchain/__init__.py` — add `from flask_migrate import Migrate` import and one new `Migrate(app, db)` line inside `create_app()`.
- **Modify:** `src/cancelchain/command.py` — change `init_db_command` body from `db.create_all()` to `flask_migrate.upgrade()`. Add `from flask_migrate import upgrade as flask_migrate_upgrade` (or similar) import.
- **Modify:** `pyproject.toml` — add `'Flask-Migrate>=4'` to `[project.dependencies]` (capitalized to match the existing `Flask`, `Flask-Caching`, `Flask-SQLAlchemy` entries; case-insensitive alphabetical order places it between `Flask-Caching` and `Flask-SQLAlchemy`).
- **Modify:** `uv.lock` — regenerated by `uv lock` after the pyproject edit.
- **Modify:** `.github/workflows/tests.yml` — add `uv run cancelchain db check` as a fifth step after the existing four (`ruff check`, `ruff format --check`, `pytest`, `mypy`). No other workflow file runs that gate sequence.
- **Modify:** `CLAUDE.md` — add a one-section note documenting the Alembic workflow: how to generate a migration (`uv run cancelchain db migrate -m "..."`), how to apply (`uv run cancelchain init` or `uv run cancelchain db upgrade`), and a reminder that `cancelchain db check` is a CI gate.

### Imports

- `database.py`: add `from sqlalchemy import MetaData` (note: existing `from sqlalchemy import (...)` block can absorb this).
- `__init__.py`: add `from flask_migrate import Migrate`.
- `command.py`: add `from flask_migrate import upgrade as flask_migrate_upgrade`.

## Test plan

- **Regression: all 236 existing tests stay green.** The Phase 8 changes shouldn't touch any test path — the `app` fixture in `tests/conftest.py` builds a per-test temporary SQLite *file* (via `NamedTemporaryFile`) and runs `db.create_all()` against it.
- **`uv run cancelchain db check` exits 0.** The initial migration matches `Base.metadata` exactly. This is the headline acceptance gate.
- **`cancelchain init` on a fresh SQLite DB produces the expected schema.** Verify by running `cancelchain init` against an empty file, then `.schema` in `sqlite3` matches what `db.create_all()` produced before. Acceptable for sqlite-specific minor differences (e.g., AUTOINCREMENT keyword presence) since both are valid SQLite forms — the test is whether the resulting DB is interoperable with the test suite.
- **Hand-review of the initial migration script.** Compare the auto-generated `upgrade()` body against `db.metadata.create_all`'s expected DDL. Look for: missing CHECK constraints, missing server defaults, type-flavor mismatches (`String(100)` vs `VARCHAR(100)`), index-naming surprises despite the convention.
- **All 5 CI steps clean.** `ruff check`, `ruff format --check`, `pytest`, `mypy`, plus the new `cancelchain db check`.

Test count: 236 (unchanged). No new tests.

## Acceptance

- `ls migrations/versions/*.py | wc -l` returns `1` (the initial migration).
- `grep -l 'Flask-Migrate' pyproject.toml` returns the path (the dep is pinned `>=4`, with the capitalized name matching the existing `Flask`, `Flask-Caching`, `Flask-SQLAlchemy` style).
- `grep -n 'naming_convention' src/cancelchain/database.py` returns matches (the convention dict + the `MetaData(naming_convention=...)` assignment).
- `grep -n 'Migrate(app, db)' src/cancelchain/__init__.py` returns one match.
- `grep -n 'flask_migrate.upgrade\|flask_migrate_upgrade' src/cancelchain/command.py` returns the new `init` body.
- `uv run cancelchain db check` exits 0 — the **headline acceptance gate**.
- `uv run cancelchain db current` reports the initial revision's hash.
- `uv run cancelchain init` on a fresh DB succeeds (verify by deleting `cancelchain.db` first, then running `init`, then running `cancelchain --help` — no exceptions).
- `uv run ruff check src tests` + `uv run ruff format --check src tests` exit 0.
- `uv run mypy` exits 0.
- `uv run pytest` exits 0; test count is 236.
- `uv run pytest --runmulti` exits 0.
- `bench/rebuild_walk_bench.py` per-step times match the Phase 7b baseline (~0.25 ms/step on local SQLite).
- `docker build --target builder -t cc-phase8 .` succeeds.

## Risks

- **Autogenerate misses non-trivial schema elements.** Alembic's `--autogenerate` reliably catches column additions, type changes, and index/unique constraint changes; it sometimes misses CHECK constraints, server defaults, and type-flavor distinctions (e.g., `Integer` vs `BigInteger` on certain dialects). Mitigation: the hand-review step is an explicit plan task. If the review surfaces a miss, edit the migration script before committing.

- **`naming_convention` renames existing constraints at the SQL level.** Today the constraints in `db.metadata` carry SA-default names. After the convention is set, all auto-generated migrations use the new canonical names (`ix_block_idx`, `uq_outflow_txid_idx`, etc.). Greenfield posture: no installed-base DB to rename anything in, so this is a free win — the initial migration captures the new canonical names and that's the starting point.

- **`cancelchain db check` false positives on metadata reflection edge cases.** Some SA features (e.g., custom `TypeDecorator`, complex `server_default` expressions) can read differently between `db.metadata` introspection and Alembic's diff engine. Mitigation: if the check reports a false positive in CI, the fix is usually an explicit `compare_type=True` / `compare_server_default=True` flag in `env.py`'s `context.configure(...)` call (both are set in the impl plan).

- **Tests using `db.create_all()` could drift from migration-built schema** at a SQL-level (column order, default expression representation, etc.). The CI `cancelchain db check` gate catches *model-level* drift. SQL-level drift between `db.create_all()` and the initial migration's `CREATE TABLE` statements would only show up if a test depends on a SQL-level detail that the two differ on. Mitigation: if this becomes a real problem, add a specific test that compares the schemas of a `db.create_all()`-created DB and a `cancelchain db upgrade`-created DB. Skip for now — the gain isn't worth the cost until evidence of actual drift surfaces.

- **Local dev with a pre-Phase-8 `cancelchain.db` file.** A developer who has been running cancelchain locally pre-Phase-8 has a SQLite file lying around with the old SA-default constraint names and no `alembic_version` table. After Phase 8 lands, `cancelchain init` against that file would fail (no version row → tries to create existing tables). Mitigation: the CLAUDE.md section documents `rm cancelchain.db` as the one-step recovery — there's no production data to preserve, and re-running `cancelchain init` after the delete recreates the schema cleanly through migrations. No `alembic stamp head` ceremony needed since there's no data to keep.

- **Flask-Migrate dependency pin.** `Flask-Migrate>=4` is broad. If a `>5.0` major version ships with breaking changes, we'd want to pin tighter. For now, `>=4` is fine — `uv.lock` pins the exact version we install, so the broad constraint doesn't actually destabilize anything.

- **`alembic.ini` log-level defaults vs project preferences.** Alembic's default `alembic.ini` is chatty. After `cancelchain db init`, may want to set `level = WARN` on the `[logger_alembic]` section to keep CLI output clean. Small cosmetic edit.

- **`env.py` needs `compare_type=True`.** Alembic's default is `compare_type=False`, which means autogenerate won't detect column-type changes (e.g., `Integer` → `BigInteger`). The impl plan flips this on in `env.py`'s `context.configure(...)` call as part of the standard project-style edits to the Flask-Migrate-generated `env.py`. Recommend setting `compare_server_default=True` at the same time for consistent behavior on default-value changes.

## Open decisions

None at design time. Brainstorming + the user's three "yes to all 3" callouts resolved:
- Flask-Migrate (not raw Alembic).
- `cancelchain init` calls `alembic upgrade head` (single source of truth).
- CI gate `cancelchain db check` is in.
- Naming convention on `Base.metadata` is in.
- Hand-review of the initial migration is an explicit plan step.

## What comes next

- **Phase 8 closed.** The ROADMAP's "Phase 7+ — Alembic migration framework" entry moves to Closed items, the framework is in place, and every future schema change rides a migration.
- **Phase 9+ — Other ROADMAP items.** Per `docs/superpowers/ROADMAP.md`: generalize materialization to all chains, cross-worker `_is_longest()` cache invalidation, batched-fetch chain walk (Phase 6.7). None of these depend on Alembic being in place; the next phase pick can be by current priority.
- **First real migration.** Whenever a schema change happens next (e.g., a column add, an index for query optimization), the workflow is: edit the model in `models.py`, run `uv run cancelchain db migrate -m "<message>"`, hand-review the generated migration, commit it. CI's `cancelchain db check` enforces that the model edit and migration revision land together.
